sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 45-year-old male with a history of diabetes type two and mild intermittent asthma, presents with right elbow pain that began three weeks ago after increasing tennis activity.
The pain is located on the outside of the elbow and is described as sharp, stabbing, and throbbing, with a severity of 8/10 when using the elbow and 3/10 when at rest.
The patient denies any trauma or recent falls.
He has tried taking Tylenol and ibuprofen, but they provide minimal relief.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of diabetes type two, mild intermittent asthma, a penicillin allergy, and a surgical history of a gallbladder removal 10 years ago.
Review of systems: The patient denies any other symptoms.
Current medications: Metformin 500mg twice a day for diabetes, Flovent 2 puffs twice a day and albuterol as needed for asthma.
Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient has a normal cardiovascular exam with a grade 3/6 systolic ejection murmur.
The musculoskeletal exam reveals moderate lateral epicondylar tenderness of the right elbow, with pain on passive range of motion and mild pain with resisted extension of the right wrist.
The right elbow x-ray shows no acute fracture or other bony abnormality.
Test Results: The right elbow x-ray shows no acute fracture or other bony abnormality.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has acute lateral epicondylitis of the right elbow, also known as tennis elbow, due to overuse likely caused by increasing tennis activity.
Problem list: Acute lateral epicondylitis of the right elbow; diabetes type two; mild intermittent asthma.
Plan:
I recommended that the patient rest their right arm as much as possible, stop tennis for 4-6 weeks, and avoid repetitive stress in the right arm.
I also prescribed ultram 50mg once a day for pain relief and ordered a brace for the right forearm.
I will see the patient in 4 weeks to reevaluate the elbow and consider additional imaging or physical therapy if needed.
Follow-up: The patient will return in 4 weeks for follow-up.
